content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Silverlight officially reached end-of-life years ago. Microsoft stopped supporting it, browsers dropped the plugin architecture it depended on, and thousands of line-of-business apps built on it were left stranded. Rewriting them in a modern stack is usually a six-figure project. That’s the exact gap OpenSilver was built to close — and unlike most tools that solve expensive problems, it costs nothing.
What OpenSilver actually is
Press enter or click to view image in full size

OpenSilver isn’t an emulator or a browser wrapper around old Silverlight binaries. It’s a ground-up reimplementation of the WPF and Silverlight APIs, compiled through WebAssembly, so the C# and XAML you already know target the browser natively. It’s distributed under the MIT license and installs as a Visual Studio extension plus a set of NuGet packages — no proprietary runtime, no seat licenses.
Building something real, not a “hello world”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

For this walkthrough I built ExpenseFlow, a small MVVM expense tracker: a bound list, a form with commands, a live-updating total, and a currency converter — using CommunityToolkit.Mvvm exactly the way you’d wire up a WPF app. That was deliberate. A framework only earns a place in a “no paid tools” toolchain if it survives contact with a real, if small, line-of-business shape — not a static demo page.
The moment that sold me: real debugging
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The single biggest gap in browser-based low-code tools is debugging. OpenSilver’s Simulator project runs your app as a native desktop process during development, which means you get an actual breakpoint, actual call stacks, and actual variable inspection — the same experience WPF developers already have muscle memory for. Then, with one project-switch, the same codebase compiles and runs as WebAssembly in a real browser tab.
Where the free line actually sits
To be precise about the “free” claim: the core SDK, the Visual Studio and VS Code extensions, the Simulator, and the WebAssembly target are all free and MIT-licensed. OpenSilver’s maintainers also offer paid enterprise migration services and support contracts for teams porting large legacy Silverlight codebases — none of which is required for anything shown in this build.
Where this fits in the bigger picture
This continues a series built around one premise: production-quality .NET UI development doesn’t require paid component libraries. Seven episodes in, that premise keeps holding — WPF, Avalonia, MAUI, and now OpenSilver, all shipped without a single commercial license.
Full video walkthrough and the ExpenseFlow source are linked below. If you’re maintaining a legacy Silverlight app right now, this is worth an afternoon of your time before you sign off on a rewrite budget.
